titleStmt.title: Letter from Peter Mooney to sister Katie, 10 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Peter Mooney

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Orphaned at age 7, Peter Mooney and his sister Katie Mooney were raised by their uncle William and his wife Mary in Caulstown. While there are no surviving enlistment papers for Peter, his War Badge Index Record shows his enlistment date as 18 October 1915. He served with the Royal Artillery and later transferred to the Royal Engineers where he was promoted to the rank of Sergeant. He served in several places at the Western Front, including Ypres, St. Omer and Passchendael. Peter and Kate were avid correspondents. This letter part of a collection of over ninety surviving letters.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
July 10th 1916 Dear Katie, I hope this letter will find you all in good health
I am in the pink & feel better than ever. The weather is very
fine out here I hope it is the same in the old country. I suppose
the meadow is mowed by this time. As I write this letter the
fair of Dunboyne is in full swing I suppose Mathy Geragthy is
bringing a few screws today. How is Paddy going on
these times. I hope things are going better now do the best
you can until I get home again. You might send me a
razor in next parcel I have only one now another comes in
handy. I hope Bridget is well of the toothache tell her to remember
me to all at home. How is old Billy he is very old now the
little (jockey) horse must be a beauty now. The hedges along
the road will get a rest this summer. I will not be there to
keep them down. I would like if they could be kept in order
somehow. I know Jonny will do his best. I had a letter from
Tommy last week he told me the foal is as wild as a
stag. I was dreaming that I saw the two of them last night.
Remember me to all the neighbours around I hope all are well.
It will be a long time before I am home again I might be here
for the finish one are giving them socks now they will never beat
us. I have no more to say at present so I say good bye & may god
bless & keep you all. Good bye. Peter Mooney.
Write soon.
Miss K. Mooney
Caulstown
Dunboyne
Co Meath
Ireland
